questions.question: What is the difference between a function declaration and a definition?
options: They are the same thing — the terms are interchangeable
A declaration provides the body; a definition just states the function exists
A declaration tells the compiler the function exists; a definition provides the body (instructions)
A declaration is in the .h file; a definition is in the .cpp file
questions.correctAnswer: A declaration tells the compiler the function exists; a definition provides the body (instructions)
questions.explanation: The note states: 'A declaration tells the compiler this function exists somewhere. A definition provides the body — the actual instructions.'

questions.question: What is the difference between parameters and arguments?
options: They are the same thing
Parameters are the variables in the declaration; arguments are the values passed at the call site
Arguments are in the declaration; parameters are passed at the call site
Parameters are for input; arguments are for output
questions.correctAnswer: Parameters are the variables in the declaration; arguments are the values passed at the call site
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Parameters are the variables in the declaration (a, b). Arguments are the values at the call site (Add(3, 4)).'

questions.question: What does the `call` instruction do at the hardware level?
options: Calls malloc to allocate stack space
Saves all registers to memory then jumps
Pushes the return address onto the stack (RSP-=8, [RSP]=RIP_next) and jumps to the function (RIP=&func)
Sends an interrupt to the OS kernel
questions.correctAnswer: Pushes the return address onto the stack (RSP-=8, [RSP]=RIP_next) and jumps to the function (RIP=&func)
questions.explanation: The note shows the three steps of call: '1. RSP = RSP - 8, 2. [RSP] = RIP_next (save return address), 3. RIP = &Add (jump to function).'

questions.question: What does the `ret` instruction do?
options: Returns the value in RAX to the OS
Pops the return address from the stack into RIP and increments RSP
Destroys the stack frame and calls the destructor
Jumps to the instruction before the call
questions.correctAnswer: Pops the return address from the stack into RIP and increments RSP
questions.explanation: The note shows: 'ret: 1. RIP = [RSP] (pop return address), 2. RSP = RSP + 8 (reclaim stack space).'

questions.question: Which register holds the return value of a function on x86-64?
options: RSP
RBP
RAX
RIP
questions.correctAnswer: RAX
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Integer/pointer returns go in RAX. Float returns in XMM0.' The caller reads RAX after ret to get the result.

questions.question: What is the stack frame prologue?
options: The first line of code in a function
push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N — saves the caller's frame pointer, establishes a new base, allocates locals
The return statement at the end of a function
The call instruction itself
questions.correctAnswer: push rbp; mov rbp, rsp; sub rsp, N — saves the caller's frame pointer, establishes a new base, allocates locals
questions.explanation: The note shows the prologue as: 'push rbp (save caller's frame pointer), mov rbp, rsp (new frame base), sub rsp, N (allocate N bytes for locals).'

questions.question: What causes a stack overflow?
options: Calling too many different functions
Recursion that exhausts the fixed-size stack — RSP crosses into unmapped guard pages
Allocating too much memory with new
Using too many local variables in a single function
questions.correctAnswer: Recursion that exhausts the fixed-size stack — RSP crosses into unmapped guard pages
questions.explanation: The note states: 'When RSP crosses into unmapped guard pages beyond the stack allocation, the MMU raises a page fault → OS delivers SIGSEGV.'

questions.question: How does Tail Call Optimization transform a recursive call?
options: It inlines the recursive function body
It replaces `call` with `jmp`, reusing the same stack frame for constant stack depth
It moves the recursion to the heap
It unrolls the recursion into a loop using templates
questions.correctAnswer: It replaces `call` with `jmp`, reusing the same stack frame for constant stack depth
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Tail Call Optimization changes call to jmp, reusing the same frame. Constant stack depth.'

questions.question: What is the main purpose of an enum?
options: To save memory by using smaller integer types
To replace magic numbers with named, self-documenting constants
To enable compile-time computation
To create type hierarchies like classes
questions.correctAnswer: To replace magic numbers with named, self-documenting constants
questions.explanation: The note states: 'An enum is a set of named integer constants. Instead of passing magic numbers, you pass names with meaning.'

questions.question: Why can't two plain enums share member names in the same scope?
options: The C++ standard forbids it for performance reasons
Plain enum values leak into the surrounding scope — two enums with 'Red' would conflict
The linker can't resolve duplicate enum names
It's a compiler bug, not a language rule
questions.correctAnswer: Plain enum values leak into the surrounding scope — two enums with 'Red' would conflict
questions.explanation: The note shows: 'enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }; enum TrafficLight { Red, Yellow, Green }; // ERROR — Red and Green already defined!'

questions.question: What are two key advantages of `enum class` over plain `enum`?
questions.correctAnswer: Strongly typed (no implicit conversion to int) and scoped (values must be accessed with scope prefix)
acceptableAnswers: no implicit int conversion and scoped
questions.explanation: The note states: 'enum class is strongly typed and scoped. Values don't leak, don't convert to int implicitly, and don't conflict with other enums.'

questions.question: How do you convert an `enum class` value to its underlying integer?
options: Just assign it — the conversion is automatic
Use `static_cast<int>(value)` or C++23's `std::to_underlying(value)`
Call `.value()` on the enum
Use `(int)value` — C-style cast
questions.correctAnswer: Use `static_cast<int>(value)` or C++23's `std::to_underlying(value)`
questions.explanation: The note shows: 'int x = static_cast<int>(Color::Red);' and mentions std::to_underlying in C++23.

questions.question: What underlying type does an enum use by default?
options: uint8_t
int
size_t
unsigned int
questions.correctAnswer: int
questions.explanation: The note states: 'By default, the underlying type is int for both enum styles.' You can specify a smaller type like uint8_t to save memory.

questions.question: Why would you specify `enum class Permission : uint8_t` instead of the default?
options: To make the enum values larger
To save memory — 1 byte instead of 4, important when enums are stored in arrays or as struct members
To enable bitwise operations
To make the enum work with templates
questions.correctAnswer: To save memory — 1 byte instead of 4, important when enums are stored in arrays or as struct members
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Use uint8_t for small enums. UE uses uint8 extensively for enum class types. This saves memory when enums are stored in arrays or as struct members.'

questions.question: What is a reference in C++?
options: A copy of a variable stored on the heap
An alias — another name for an existing variable, not a copy and not a pointer
A smart pointer that automatically deallocates
A const pointer that can never be null
questions.correctAnswer: An alias — another name for an existing variable, not a copy and not a pointer
questions.explanation: The note states: 'A reference is an alias — another name for an existing variable. It's not a copy and not a pointer. It IS the original variable.'

questions.question: What happens when you assign to a reference?
options: The reference changes to point to the new value's address
The original variable the reference aliases is modified
A copy of the new value is made, leaving the original unchanged
The program crashes because references are immutable
questions.correctAnswer: The original variable the reference aliases is modified
questions.explanation: The note states: 'ref = 100; // Changes original to 100. original = 200; // Changes ref to 200 — they're the SAME variable.'

questions.question: What is a key difference between references and pointers?
options: References can be null; pointers cannot
References must be initialized when declared and cannot be reassigned; pointers can be null and reassigned
References are stored on the heap; pointers on the stack
References are slower than pointers
questions.correctAnswer: References must be initialized when declared and cannot be reassigned; pointers can be null and reassigned
questions.explanation: The note states: 'References must be bound at declaration, cannot be null, and cannot be reassigned. Pointers can be null, reassigned, and require explicit dereference.'

questions.question: What is pass-by-reference used for?
options: To make a copy of arguments for the function
To avoid copying large objects — the function receives a reference to the original data
To enable function overloading
To make the function run faster by using CPU registers
questions.correctAnswer: To avoid copying large objects — the function receives a reference to the original data
questions.explanation: The note describes references enabling 'zero-copy parameter passing.' The function works directly on the caller's data.

questions.question: How are references typically implemented at the hardware level?
options: As a special CPU register
As a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores an address
As a hash table lookup
As a direct alias in the symbol table with no runtime representation
questions.correctAnswer: As a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores an address
questions.explanation: The Systems Connection note states: 'References are typically implemented as pointers under the hood — the compiler stores an address.'

questions.question: How many distinct meanings does the `static` keyword have in C++?
questions.explanation: The note explicitly states: 'One Keyword, Four Meanings.' The four contexts are: member variable, member function, local variable, and global variable/function.

questions.question: What does a static member variable do?
options: Creates a separate copy for each class instance
Creates one variable shared across ALL instances of the class
Prevents the variable from being modified
Makes the variable only visible in the .cpp file
questions.correctAnswer: Creates one variable shared across ALL instances of the class
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Static Member Variables — One Copy, Shared By All. One variable shared by ALL Soldier objects.'

questions.question: What is the Static Initialization Order Fiasco?
options: When static variables are initialized in the wrong order due to undefined construction order across translation units — causing crashes before main()
When a static variable is initialized twice
When static functions are called in the wrong order
When inline static variables conflict with out-of-class definitions
questions.correctAnswer: When static variables are initialized in the wrong order due to undefined construction order across translation units — causing crashes before main()
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Global static objects across multiple .cpp files have unspecified construction order. One reads another that hasn't been constructed yet — crash before main().'

questions.question: How does Meyer's Singleton (function-local static) solve the init order fiasco?
options: It uses mutexes to serialize initialization
It defers initialization to the first function call, eliminating ordering dependencies between translation units
It moves all static variables to the heap
It uses compiler attributes to force initialization order
questions.correctAnswer: It defers initialization to the first function call, eliminating ordering dependencies between translation units
questions.explanation: The note shows: 'static std::string& GetLogPath() { static std::string path = "/var/log/app.log"; return path; } — Now order doesn't matter, path is constructed when GetLogPath() is first called.'

questions.question: What does C++17 `inline static` allow you to do?
options: Mark a static variable as always inlined by the optimizer
Define a static member variable directly in the class definition — no out-of-class .cpp definition required
Share a static variable across DLL boundaries
Make a static variable thread-local
questions.correctAnswer: Define a static member variable directly in the class definition — no out-of-class .cpp definition required
questions.explanation: The note states: 'C++17 inline static — No Out-of-Class Definition Needed. Definition AND declaration in one place!'

questions.question: What does `static` at file/namespace scope do?
options: Makes the variable shared across all .cpp files
Gives the variable/function internal linkage — visible only within this translation unit (.cpp file)
Makes the variable initialize before main()
Prevents the variable from being modified
questions.correctAnswer: Gives the variable/function internal linkage — visible only within this translation unit (.cpp file)
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Static Global Variables/Functions — Internal Linkage. The variable/function is private to this translation unit.'

questions.question: Since which C++ standard is static local variable initialization thread-safe?
questions.explanation: The note states: 'Thread safety (C++11+): Static local initialization is guaranteed thread-safe. The compiler inserts a hidden lock.'
